definition: A training method that shapes a representation by pulling matching pairs together and pushing non-matching pairs apart in the vector space, the dominant recipe for training an embedding model and the reason distance comes to mean similarity. The quality of the negatives is decisive: hard negatives, items that look similar but are not the right match, are what force the model to learn fine distinctions.

body.text: Contrastive learning trains a model by comparison rather than by predicting a fixed label. The model is shown pairs and taught a single geometric rule: examples that belong together (an anchor and a positive) should map to nearby points, and examples that do not (the anchor and one or more negatives) should map far apart. Repeated over many pairs, this sculpts a vector space in which proximity encodes similarity. It is the dominant recipe for training an embedding model, and so it is the reason dense retrieval and semantic search work at all.

The training signal is a contrastive loss. A common form, InfoNCE, frames each step as a soft retrieval problem: given an anchor, the model must score its true positive above a batch of negatives, and the loss falls as the positive's similarity rises relative to the rest. What matters most is that the quality of the negatives is decisive: easy, obviously-unrelated negatives teach little, while hard negatives, items that look similar but are not the right match, force the model to learn fine distinctions, which is why mining hard negatives is a central concern. A practical trick is in-batch negatives, treating the other examples in a mini-batch as negatives so a large batch supplies many comparisons cheaply.

Contrastive learning matters because it solves the data problem that supervised labeling cannot. Pairs are far easier to obtain than category labels, and many arise for free: a question and its accepted answer, a passage and its title, two crops of one image, a sentence and its translation. In self-supervised vision, methods like SimCLR build positives by augmenting the same image two different ways. This abundance of pair data is what lets embedding models train on enormous corpora and generalize across domains. The same idea also trains cross-encoder rerankers, which learn to score a relevant query-document pair above irrelevant ones.

The payoff is visible everywhere on this site, because the contrastively trained voyage-3 model is what makes its vector space meaningful. The reason a query retrieves passages that share its meaning but not its words, the reason near-duplicate concepts in the AI-grown taxonomy collapse onto one canonical concept by embedding proximity, and the reason the clustering and rotation layouts group related items, all trace back to a space organized by a contrastive objective: similar things were pulled together and dissimilar things pushed apart during training, and the site reads that geometry back out.

image.alt: A two-dimensional embedding space with an anchor point a1. A green arrow points from a1 to a single matching point b1 (a positive), and several red arrows point from a1 to other points b2 through b5 (negatives) scattered farther away.
image.caption: Contrastive learning shapes an embedding space by comparison. For each anchor it pulls the matching example (the positive b1, green) closer and pushes the non-matching ones (the negatives b2 to b5, red) farther away. Trained with many in-batch negatives like this, the model learns the single rule that distance should mean dissimilarity, which is what makes the resulting embeddings effective for dense retrieval and semantic search.
image.credit: Nils Reimers and the sentence-transformers contributors
